scenarioStory.history: 17-year-old male pulled from Lake Harmon by his friends after an estimated 4-6 minutes of submersion in cold water. Group of teenagers were jumping off a dock into the lake. Witnesses report he hit the water and did not resurface. Friends pulled him out after approximately 5 minutes. Water temperature is approximately 12°C (54°F). Bystanders started rescue breaths on shore.
scenarioStory.discussion: Apply MATC Drowning protocol including ABC approach. Manage the hypothermic drowning patient without precipitating VF. Recognize immersion pulmonary edema and use CPAP as treatment. Understand when c-spine precautions are indicated in drowning. Document submersion time and water temperature for termination-of-resuscitation decisions
scenarioStory.course: Arrival — Submersion Victim with Gasping Respirations: Adolescent drowning victim. ABC approach (not CAB) is critical in drowning — airway and breathing come first because the primary insult is hypoxic. The patient has spontaneous gasping respirations and a pulse but is hypoxic and bradycardic. Cold water exposure adds hypothermia to manage. Mechanism (jumping, not diving) makes c-spine injury less likely but should still be considered. → Default Path — Improvement with Oxygenation and Warming: With aggressive BVM support, drying, and warming, the patient's SpO2 climbs, HR comes up, and consciousness improves. He may be able to be managed with high-flow oxygen or CPAP instead of continued BVM. Still requires transport for observation.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Pulled from a lake after estimated 4-6 minute submersion

scenarioEvents.name: Arrival — Submersion Victim with Gasping Respirations
scenarioEvents.description: Adolescent drowning victim. ABC approach (not CAB) is critical in drowning — airway and breathing come first because the primary insult is hypoxic. The patient has spontaneous gasping respirations and a pulse but is hypoxic and bradycardic. Cold water exposure adds hypothermia to manage. Mechanism (jumping, not diving) makes c-spine injury less likely but should still be considered.

scenarioEvents.name: Default Path — Improvement with Oxygenation and Warming
scenarioEvents.description: With aggressive BVM support, drying, and warming, the patient's SpO2 climbs, HR comes up, and consciousness improves. He may be able to be managed with high-flow oxygen or CPAP instead of continued BVM. Still requires transport for observation.

checklist.title: Use ABC approach (Airway-Breathing-Circulation), not CAB, for drowning
Suction airway of water without abdominal thrusts
Begin 2-person BVM with PEEP and high-flow oxygen
Remove wet clothing and apply warm blankets
Handle the hypothermic patient gently to avoid precipitating VF
Apply cardiac monitor and watch for hypothermic dysrhythmias
Consider CPAP for immersion pulmonary edema if mental status permits
Transport all drowning victims for observation
Consider c-spine precautions based on mechanism
Document submersion time and water temperature
